title: Letting People Leave Who Couldn't Hold You Honestly
dek: You carry the weight of those who once promised they’d stay but couldn’t hold your truth. This isn’t about failure or shame; it’s about the body’s whispers—the clenching masseter, the tight levator scapulae by dawn, the slow breath stuck beneath your diaphragm. Here, you meet the pain and the release with herbal allies and somatic insight, learning from Karla McLaren and Gabor Maté how to honor your fury as a messenger of what was never meant to be held.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Body’s Quiet Witness: Noticing the Weight of Holding What Wasn’t Meant to Stay

You wake in the early hours, the pale gray light seeping through the cracked blinds, and the tightness in your upper trapezius is an old, familiar ache. It’s not just stress—it’s the residue of holding someone who never held you honestly. The muscle fibers along your neck and shoulders coil like a spring compressed too long, the levator scapulae twitching with a low hum of tension beneath the skin. Your breath is shallow, caught in the diaphragm, as if the very act of inhaling fullness is a betrayal of the emptiness you carry. This is the body’s true ledger, the ledger that no conversation or apology ever balances.

Karla McLaren reminds us that anger and grief are not to be feared but understood as forms of communication—bodily messages telling you what your nervous system can’t yet say in words. What happens when you refuse to let go of someone who couldn’t hold you honestly? The masseter tightens, your jaw clenches, and the fascia around your temporal bone stiffens, locking your skull like a vault. You become a container for their absence, a living monument to what was never fully given.

The body remembers. Robert Augustus Masters calls this “disowned parts” of ourselves—those shards of experience lodged in muscle and fascia, waiting not for erasure but for acknowledgment. The pain in your shoulder blades, the heaviness in your chest, the sour taste of exhaustion in your mouth at midnight: all these are your body’s truth-tellers. They say, “I was here. I was held back. I was not enough.”

The truth is, you were enough. The failure was never yours to carry. But letting someone leave who couldn't hold you honestly requires more than mental permission—it requires a somatic surrender to the biology of loss and release.

## The Jaw Remembers: How Holding On Becomes a Physical Habit

You notice it first in the way your teeth grind softly at night, a rhythmic, unconscious gnashing that wears at the enamel and reverberates through your skull. The masseter, the muscle that closes your jaw, becomes your nightly companion in grief. This is where anger and disappointment often lodge, a clenched fortress that the nervous system uses to protect the heart’s vulnerability.

Bessel van der Kolk’s work on trauma teaches us that the body stores what the mind cannot process. Your jaw, your neck, your shoulders—they are repositories of the relational trauma of being held half-truths and broken promises. The tension here is a silent scream, a hidden fury that you were never allowed to express openly. It festers in the temporomandibular joint, where the mandible meets the skull, causing subtle misalignments and chronic discomfort.

You can feel it if you place your fingertips on the masseter and gently open and close your mouth. Notice any spots that feel denser, warmer, or more tender than others. That is where your body is still holding on. It’s not just muscle; it’s an archive of unspoken words and unmet needs.

In somatic practice, the jaw is a gateway. Releasing it is not about forcing relaxation but inviting movement and expression. You might try a simple practice: place your tongue on the roof of your mouth, just behind your front teeth, and open your jaw slightly, letting it drop into a soft, easy hinge. Hum gently, feeling the vibration in your skull and throat. This hum is a signal to your nervous system that it is safe to begin unwinding.

Herbs like skullcap ([Gaia Herbs PRO Sleep Formula](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004J32XIG?tag=spankyspinola-20)) and chamomile can support this process. They have a calming effect on the nervous system, easing the tightness in the muscles and allowing the fascia to soften. Skullcap, in particular, can help soothe the nervous system’s overdrive, which is often the hidden driver behind jaw clenching.

## The Diaphragm’s Quiet Collapse: Breathing Through Abandonment

You live in a culture that prizes “holding it together,” so when someone leaves you—especially if they left dishonestly or without true regard—the impact is visceral. Your diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs, collapses inward, shrinking the space for breath. You find yourself taking short, shallow breaths in the early evening, when the light softens and the silence grows loud.

Gabor Maté speaks profoundly about the importance of the breath in healing relational wounds. He emphasizes that trauma is stored in the body and that reclaiming breath is reclaiming self. Your breath is the first step in letting go—not just of the person, but of the body patterns that came from holding onto their absence.

The diaphragm is connected to the psoas muscle, the deep core muscle that holds your spine and hips together. When you are anxious or grieving, the psoas often tightens, pulling your lower ribs inward and further compressing the diaphragm. This creates a cycle: shallow breath leads to psoas tension, which leads to more breath restriction.

To begin loosening this, you can use an embodied breath practice. Lie on your back on a soft blanket, knees bent, feet flat on the floor. Place one hand on your belly and the other on your chest. Inhale slowly through your nose, imagining your belly filling like a balloon, pushing your hand upward, while your chest remains still. Exhale through pursed lips, gently pulling your belly down. Do this for five minutes, allowing the diaphragm to stretch and the psoas to release tension.

Herbs like wild yam ([Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20)) and astragalus have been used traditionally in Chinese medicine to support the respiratory system and strengthen the body’s qi, or vital energy. These herbs can help rebuild your resilience after the exhausting work of emotional release.

## When the Psoas Locks: The Body’s Nervous System Holds Grief Like Armor

The psoas muscle is often called the “muscle of the soul” because of its deep connection to our survival nervous system. It wraps around your lumbar spine and attaches to the inside of your femur, anchoring your body and helping you stand upright. When someone leaves you without holding you honestly, the psoas contracts reflexively, pulling your body into a protective curl.

Resmaa Menakem’s work on racialized trauma and somatic healing highlights how the psoas can store unspoken trauma. Even if your loss isn’t racialized, the principle holds: your body locks down parts of itself to protect the vulnerable core. The psoas tightness is often accompanied by a sense of being “stuck,” unable to move forward because the body is still in fight-or-flight mode.

You can feel your psoas by lying on your back and sliding your fingers just inside the front of your hip bones, pressing gently. Notice any areas of hardness or tenderness. That is your body’s archive of tension, a muscle that has held your grief in place.

A somatic release practice involves gentle movement and breath. Try this: stand with feet hip-width apart and place one hand on your lower belly. As you inhale, imagine the psoas lengthening, the belly expanding. As you exhale, gently bend forward from the hips, keeping your spine long. Repeat slowly, with awareness of the sensations in your lower belly and hips.

Herbal allies for psoas tension include valerian root ([Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20)) and passionflower. Both have calming effects on muscle spasm and nervous system overactivation, supporting your body’s natural ability to let go.

## The Trapezius and Levator Scapulae: The Shoulders as a Ledger of Bearing Burdens

When you hold onto someone who couldn’t hold you, your shoulders carry the unspoken weight. The upper trapezius and levator scapulae muscles tighten and knot, creating an almost permanent brace around your neck and upper back. These muscles are intimately linked to the vagus nerve, which regulates your parasympathetic nervous system.

Bessel van der Kolk writes extensively about how trauma contracts these muscles, creating chronic pain and limiting your capacity to relax. The result is a body that feels heavy, burdened, as if you are physically carrying the weight of someone else’s absence on your back.

You can notice this in the late afternoon, when the sun tilts low and the world feels as heavy as your shoulders. The skin might smell faintly of sweat and dried sage you burned earlier, the musk of effort and exhaustion mingling in the air.

To begin unwinding these areas, try a gentle self-massage with a warm herbal oil infused with arnica and St. John’s wort. These herbs have anti-inflammatory properties and can soothe the fascia, the connective tissue that wraps your muscles like a spider’s web.

Find the base of your skull and slowly work your fingers down the trapezius, paying close attention to any knots or areas that feel tender. Breathe into these spots, inviting the muscles to soften and lengthen.

## The Nervous System’s Role: Why Your Body Clings to the Past

The nervous system is the ultimate keeper of relational memory. It is not your fault that your body clings to the echoes of someone’s departure or dishonesty. This is not pathology, this is biology. Dr. Gabor Maté teaches that the nervous system remembers trauma as a survival mechanism. It holds patterns of contraction and vigilance to protect you from future pain.

When a person who promised to hold you honestly leaves, your nervous system experiences a rupture—a shattering of safety that it can only respond to by tightening and guarding. This is why you might feel jumpy, hypervigilant, or emotionally numb at times. These are not moral failures or weaknesses; they are the body’s way of keeping you alive.

Resmaa Menakem emphasizes the importance of “resourcing” your nervous system with somatic practices that bring you back into the body’s present moment. Feeling your feet on the ground, noticing the temperature of the air on your skin, or sensing the rhythm of your heartbeat are ways to calm the nervous system’s fight-or-flight response.

You might lie down on the earth in the late morning sun, the ground warm but not hot, and place your bare feet on the soil. Feel the roughness of the dirt against your skin, the pull of gravity grounding your body. This is an act of reclaiming your body’s wisdom and safety.

## How the Jaw, Diaphragm, and Psoas Create a Chain of Tension

The body is a connected system. The masseter’s tension in your jaw often links to the diaphragm’s collapse, which in turn tightens the psoas. This chain reaction is a physical map of your emotional experience. When one part holds grief or anger, the others respond in kind.

Think of the fascia as a web that connects muscle to muscle, bone to skin, and mind to body. When you hold onto someone who couldn’t hold you honestly, this web tightens, restricting movement and creating chronic pain.

Somatic teacher Pema Chödrön speaks to the power of “leaning into” discomfort with curiosity. When you notice this chain of tension, rather than resisting it, allow your awareness to follow it. Notice how your jaw feels as you breathe into your belly. Notice how your psoas responds as you soften your shoulders.

This embodied awareness can begin to unravel the chain, allowing new patterns of release and safety to form.

## The Time of Night: When the Body’s Stories Grow Loudest

There is a particular cruelty in nighttime—the hours between 2 a.m. and 4 a.m.—when the world is quiet, and the body’s stories rise to the surface. Your body smells faintly of lavender and cedarwood, the herbs you burned before bed to soothe your racing mind. Yet beneath this scent is the musk of old wounds, the layers of stored grief that no one sees.

This is when the diaphragm feels strangled, the psoas clenched, the jaw locked hard. The body’s nervous system is in a vulnerable state, its defenses lowered, and the unspoken truths of loss can flood through.

Thich Nhat Hanh teaches that in these moments, we can practice “holding the pain with tenderness.” This means allowing the sensations to be just as they are without judgment or resistance. You might lie on your left side, the side of the heart, and place a warm flaxseed pack over your ribs to soothe the diaphragm and soften the breath.

The physical warmth of the pack encourages blood flow to the area, easing muscle tension and inviting rest. This practice is not about forcing the pain away but about turning toward it with a steady, compassionate presence.

## The Herbal Allies: Medicine for the Body Holding On

The body’s pain and tension from relational abandonment are not just metaphorical; they are physical realities that require real medicine. Herbs have been used for millennia to support the body’s natural healing processes, especially in the realm of nervous system regulation and muscle relaxation.

Skullcap and chamomile, as mentioned, soothe the nervous system and ease muscle tension in the jaw and neck. Their gentle sedative effects help quiet the mind and body, making space for emotional release.

Wild yam and astragalus support the respiratory system and strengthen qi, helping to rebuild vitality after emotional exhaustion. Valerian root and passionflower relax the psoas and reduce nervous system hyperarousal.

You might prepare a tincture blend combining these herbs, taking small doses throughout the day to support your body’s unwinding process. The herbs are not magic, but they are serious medicine—companion allies in the slow work of letting go.

## The Practice of Letting Go: A Somatic Ritual

Letting go of someone who couldn’t hold you honestly is not a single act but a process that unfolds over time in the body and mind. You can create a somatic ritual to honor this process.

Begin by finding a quiet space in your home, perhaps in the morning when the light is soft and the air smells of damp earth and fresh herbs. Sit comfortably, feet grounded on the floor. Close your eyes and take a slow, deep breath into your belly. Place one hand on your heart and the other on your belly.

As you breathe, invite yourself to notice any areas of tension—jaw, diaphragm, shoulders, hips—and breathe into those places. Imagine your breath as a wave, gently washing over the tightness, softening it.

After several breaths, open your eyes and take a small handful of dried lavender or rose petals—herbs that carry the energy of gentleness and self-compassion. Hold them in your hands and speak aloud, “I release what was never mine to hold. I honor my truth. I am enough.”

Let the petals fall to the ground or into a bowl, symbolizing your willingness to let go.

## FAQ: Questions from the Body and Mind

**Q: Why does my body feel physically stuck when someone leaves me?**  
A: Your nervous system perceives relational abandonment as a threat to survival. Muscles like the psoas, trapezius, and masseter contract to protect vulnerable areas, creating physical tension that can feel like being stuck. This is not a failure on your part; it is biology responding to trauma.

**Q: How can I safely release jaw tension without causing more pain?**  
A: Start with gentle, mindful movements—softly opening and closing your mouth, humming to create vibration, and using calming herbs like skullcap and chamomile. Avoid forcing the jaw open or using excessive pressure. If pain persists, consult a somatic therapist or bodyworker familiar with TMJ issues.

**Q: Are herbal supplements safe to take with other medications?**  
A: Herbs like valerian, skullcap, and passionflower can interact with medications, especially those affecting the nervous system. Always consult with a knowledgeable herbalist or healthcare provider before beginning any new herbal regimen.

**Q: Can somatic practices really change how I feel emotionally?**  
A: Yes. Your body holds emotional memories, and by engaging in somatic practices, you help your nervous system process and release stored tension. This can lead to profound shifts in how you experience grief, anger, and loss.

**Q: What if I’m afraid to let someone leave because of loneliness?**  
A: Fear and loneliness are real and valid. But holding on to someone who couldn’t be honest with you creates a longer-term cost to your body and soul. Somatic practices, grounding, and herbal support can help build your resilience and capacity to be with yourself in healing.

**Q: How long does it take to heal from holding someone who couldn’t hold me?**  
A: Healing is nonlinear and unique to you. Some days you will feel progress, others setbacks. Trust the body’s wisdom and be patient. Consistent somatic work and herbal support can accelerate the process, but there is no fixed timeline.

---

Your body has been carrying the story of being held and not held honestly. This story is written in muscle, fascia, breath, and nervous system patterns. It is not a story of failure or shame. It is a story of survival—and now, with awareness, somatic care, and herbal allies, it can become a story of release and renewal. You do not have to carry what was never yours to carry. The body knows how to let go, if you will listen.
